text:
We installed this TV this morning in our den to replace a flat screen that had died. Installation went fairly well, although it wasn't as straightforward as it could have been. I think a lot of manufacturers assume a level of electronic experience that not everyone has. Best advice I can give on that front is to look at a TV you already have and try to follow along with the enclosed instructions.
We have satellite where we are, and for some reason the TV wants to do a 30 minute scan for signals. It wouldn't let us override that process, so be prepared.
The picture has very vibrant color and clarity. We are watching the Golf Channel at the moment and when they show the ball you can see the blades of grass individually around it.
Nice sleek frame and body and it easily attached to the wall bracket we had.
The actual listing for this item needs some work as it is very low on specifics. All in all a very nice TV with a fabulous picture.
I've tried SEVERAL times to program channels. Still cannot change channel by using the numbers on the remote. I will be returning this item.

I know that this product is relatively inexpensive; however, there are flaws that cannot be overlooked.  The picture color is poor.  There is a blue haze in most of the picture.  When taking video, there is a sound that is like an old style movie projector that is superimposed.  I do not think that it's a feature :-/ Finally, unlike the flashes on mobile phones, this flash has to charge, which is not conducive to children. The flash also totally washes out the picture.
I got this to go with a Kindle I purchased for my Mom. It is way nicer than the cover I bought for myself. Very good quality for the cost. Protective, stylish and easy to put on.

UPDATE: After only a few months of use, the battery has severely degraded to about half of its original capacity. The worst part is, rather than deplete in a linear fashion, the battery seems to flatline at around 30%, sending my laptop into a sudden power off without warning.
Thankfully I haven't lost any data or anything due to this yet, but it is very frustrating and could be dangerous to my computer's hard drive (I'm using an SSD, but a sudden power loss during a write operation could corrupt the storage).
I'm going to reach out and see if there's anything we can do about this, and I will update my review accordingly.
This thing breathed new life into an old Lenovo X201 tablet someone gave me. I'm not sure if it was necessary or not, but I used the Lenovo tool to calibrate the new battery (it runs it down to nothing and then trickles back up to 100%), and now it runs like a champ.
Easily gets close to 5 hours of running time, and this laptop is a first generation i7 (not known for being energy efficient). I've only owned it about 10 days so far, so I can't speak for longevity down the road, but I'm hoping it will keep up, as my family is really enjoying using the tablet pen for drawing untethered.
A friend of mine built me a computer using this. Works great!
This is the third version I have had. I love them.

Initially when I received this HP MicroSD card I was impressed - speed was decent, price was great, it worked well. I formatted it in my computer, ran a few tests then installed it with a security camera.

For 3 weeks I had no issues, the card was written to and read from without issue. During week 4, I started noticing glitching. Initially I thought it could be the camera until I reviewed the recorded footage and it would freeze during playback - whether played through the camera or my computer. I reformatted and installed into the camera again.

Over the next several weeks the card became less and less reliable. It would record part of the time then freeze. Playback would freeze, play for 30 seconds then freeze again.

By week 8 this card completed froze and I could do nothing with it. It won’t format, read nor write. While the price is low on this product - you’ll get what you pay for here. Spend a little more and go with a reliable MicroSD card.

This is a perfect example of why I wait a few months of testing a product prior to writing my review. Most products will work right out of the package, but none of us like when products die immediately outside of the return window - and that’s exactly what you can expect from this card.

We recently moved in to a new house and needed this mount to install a TV on our patio. I measured the VESA holes on our TV and knew this would work prior to ordering so that was not an issue.

This will hold a 55” Visio TV from a vaulted ceiling.

Installation is not as straightforward as I have experienced with other mounts though not terribly difficult either. The issues with installation really come from limited space to work to attach the parts together and there not being great instructions.

A few tips we found as we installed that will help you - first locate your ceiling joist stud to mount into. Then find the distance from the wall where your TV can swivel both left and right to the farthest point you desire - then mark your spot.

As with all mounting brackets you want to mount into a stud with as many bolts as possible.

In terms of use this works but there are features that just should be more user friendly. For instance if the height is in the highest position the cables will be pinched in place and can not be moved at all - the cable pass through should have a notch so this is not an issue as it is never good to have cables being pinched.

In addition if you want to either swivel or tilt the TV a hex key is required instead of being able to do this with a finger tightening knob like most mounts - this is definitely the worst design flaw in my opinion. This issue has left us unable to rotate our TVs mount that is “able to swivel” when we’ve had guests at our house due to not being able to locate the correct sized hex key. Ugh.

This mount works, but I would really only recommend it for locations where the user does not intend to alter the tilt or direction of the TVs screen very often.
Bought this for my husband as a gift.  No sound!!! Don't have a printer to return.
Can't run a farm or household with these beauties. Great for all kinds of jobs.

I have tested and reviewed several of these digital photo frames over the last several years and this one is fairly mediocre.

First of all the resolution is 720p - while your images will be visible they will not be crisp and clear. As a photographer and graphic designer this is more obvious to me than others but it’s noticeable.

The overall design is clunky. The cord is both short and sticks out of the back. The only options for placement are the cheap stand which allow it to sit either vertically or horizontally on a flat surface. This can not be hung.

The iOS app is usable though I wouldn’t say it’s great. Due to not being a fan of allowing these types of devices on to my internet network I utilized the SD card option loaded it and played. I am always more concerned about security than ease of access.

I was able to load media in this way without an issue.

This looks ok but if you are looking to invest in a digital photo frame to really enjoy your photos I recommend looking at other products.

[[VIDEOID:8bed6d79d90a43dd042702aefe52b20a]] I wanted to love this phone so badly. Now that LG has left the phone market, I wanted to know there are still flagship killers like Oneplus out there to take on the bullies of wireless phones. And on paper, there's a LOT to love about this phone. Let's mention the good things first:
- As a photographer, I really like the Hasselblad cameras that include a very capable manual mode. See the included photos taken with the phone, no fake bokeh effects. That's just the natural blurring DOF of the lens and sensor.
- As someone who is picky about user experience I absolutely LOVE the silent/vibrate/ring physical switch. All smartphones need that, in my opinion.
- CPU/GPU benchmarks are awesome, right up there with flagships that cost more.
- Coming from the Galaxy S10's terrible in-screen fingerprint reader, this one renews my faith in fp readers.
- Display is beautiful and crisp.
- Battery is surprisingly good.

In a nutshell, the hardware is great! If they just throw a stock Android OS on here, it would be perfect! But sadly, no... they didn't. And the software is TERRIBLE. Chock full of bugs.
Some examples (some of which I tried to capture on video):
1) Google Voice call redirects are broken.
I've been using GV as my phone number for nearly a decade and never had a single problem like this before. The stock dialer does not care what you set GV to, it will IGNORE your outgoing call redirect requests. No matter what you set it to, it uses the carrier number to call out. Even if you select "ask before each call", the phone is so excited to dial out it will show you the option to pick, but in the background IT STARTED CALLING ALREADY and clearly doesn't care what you want. To be fair, this started with the Android 12 update on the Oneplus 9 series as well, so it's a Oneplus problem in general, it seems.

2) Android Auto pop ups make no sense.
When you plug the phone into a USB port, it will prompt you with three options. Charge, Transfer Files / Android Auto, and Transfer Photos. When I plugged it into my car with AA, I see it starting on my car's display, but the pop up still appears on the phone. Naturally, I picked option 2, since it says android auto. It disconnects. So I unplug and try again, and leave it on charge, which seems to work. For the sake of testing, I also tried transfer photos, and it also worked. It seems that THE ONLY OPTION THAT DOESN'T WORK is the one that says Android Auto on it! Seriously?! Is it opposite day?! But that's ok, my car radio also supports wireless Android Auto (uses Wifi direct instead of a cable), so that should avoid the problem right?

3) Wireless Android Auto breaks Wifi's ability to reconnect to known networks.
I know what you're thinking... come on, right? How many Google features can Oneplus break at a time. Apparently a lot. When using Android Auto over the wireless connection, it actually works great, but then the phone seems to give up ever wanting to connect to my known wifi networks again. I get to work or return home and found out that I've been using cellular data the entire time, unless I manually go into Wifi settings and click join network. If I leave the coverage area or toggle wifi off/on, it won't reconnect. I have to manually connect it in settings each time or be stuck using my data plan. A reboot fixes the problem and the wifi reconnects as expected afterwards... until the next time I take the car somewhere and the problem comes back.

4) The 3.3x Telephoto camera has its own color science.
It doesn't match the others. The main camera is actually quite good, and the wide is decent as well. The telephoto is a bit less vibrant and noticeably more dull on warm color themes. I though these were Hasselblad tuned and calibrated to have professional color science and detail? This isn't a major issue, it just seems surprising. Especially when you point the camera at something that is too dark for the limited exposure capability of the 3.3x tele lens, and it jumps to a zoomed in version of the main camera lens instead. Seriously- see my video for a demonstration of that. You see the color suddenly shift into something that looks very different whenever it changes. The main zoomed in actually looks better, and it has enough native pixels to resolve a decent image... which then begs the question of why even have a dedicated 3.3x tele lens?

4) The notifications in the shade don't let you jump to the application... only individual messages. For example, if I have a bunch of Gmail messages, I can see them in the shade and click them individually to go to them each. But I can't just go to Gmail and see my inbox. Sometimes I want to see my inbox and decide what to respond to first. I have to either go to an individual message, or back out and find the Gmail icon to click it directly.

All in all, the phone is usable but I just can't shake the feeling that the OS is half baked and not ready for release yet. The Android Auto issues and Google Voice ones are so major to the way I use my phone on a daily basis, I might prefer to go back to a device from 3 years ago than continue using this one. It just feels unpredictable and unreliable in its current state. I really do hope Oneplus fixes those bugs soon, because the hardware really is nice. Or better yet, give us the option to run a stock Android without all this OOS nonsense. The customizations aren't a selling point, they are detrimental at best and downright BREAK CERTAIN FUNCTIONALITY at worst. I will gladly change my review and update the rating if an update that addresses these issues comes around. Come on, Oneplus. We know you can do better than this on a phone that costs this much.

I’m not satisfied with this product, it stoped working for no particular reason. Lost very important data on it. I have experience with memory cards and have some that are over 7 years old and they still continue to operate. I usually use SanDisk brand but gave this brand a try to my disappointment therefore I can’t give a good review and cannot recommend it.

I got 2 of these based on all the descriptions and reviews. I needed them for outside in my back yard as a noise deterrent to the bear issue we have.  They are well made, but not really what they are described to be. According to the manual they shouldn't get that wet, especially if you are using them with a cord. The other problems are that if you unplug them and then plug them back in again, you have to manually turn them back on and retune them. Total pain in the butt for the price. If I had kept the boxes, I would have returned these after a couple of days.
It's very specific about the length of screws that will fit, but for some reason doesn't include its own screws ... why?! Come on, Apple...

Large flat screen TVs require special mounting brackets in order to safely hang them on the wall.

We used this bracket to hang our new 75 inch Sony TV. The bracket itself has worked well. We did end up utilizing the extension bracket which gave us added mobility in locating the TV in the center of the wall. Due to our hanging the TV on an interior wall that had a narrow gap between the drywall and cinder block I had to get special wall anchors to support the weight of the TV.

Due to how this bracket is designed it can be difficult to access ports, such as HDMI and USB, once the TV is hung upon the wall. It can be done but someone must unlock it by pulling the straps and hold the bottom of the TV away from the wall as someone else navigates wires below. It is definitely not easily accomplished.

This is definitely a stable TV mount - even for large TVs, it’s just not the easiest to connect accessories once the TV is hung.

There is limited tilt mobility but enough to adjust for viewing angle.

This is a decent TV mount for large TVs but could be better.

My old cover finally fell apart. This is a better replacement than the one I had. Well made and attractive. It's a little hard to get your Kindle into and it has to be seated just right or the cover doesn't close. Otherwise it's the bomb.

My previous model was an older model Viewsonic (don't recall the exact model, but it was an 800x600 one), and I had a very positive experience with it. I use this both for home entertainment and the occasional work-related presentation, so when my old one broke (fell off a table), I decided to go with another Viewsonic.

But I've got to admit, something seems to have went wrong with this model. I love using HDMI to my laptop to project vs VGA, however the first day I did this, when my laptop went to "sleep", the projector started going crazy. Waking up the laptop put it into some sort of loop, it would try to power on, the laptop would report there is no external monitor, the projector would shut off, the laptop would detect something is there just as it was powering down, projector would try to power up again, repeat. I unplugged the HDMI, the laptop went back to normal, but the projector would continue doing this until I pulled the power.

After that, I just made sure not to let the HDMI sleep when using the projector, and its been working OK. Wrote this off as a silly quirk, the picture quality is better than my old one so I'd just live with it. Until the day I was using it for a presentation at a fundraising dinner full of guests. The thing shut off every 10-15 minutes, apparently overheating. The little red light would come on and refuse to let me power it back on for a couple of minutes at a time. I had used my old Viewsonic in exactly the same situation and had NEVER had a problem like this before! After leaving it unplugged for a while, I was able to get it to come on long enough to make it through the presentation, but a couple of days later it did the same thing at another location, and I was forced to rent a backup projector.

Mind you, I'm in my third month of using the projector, I've clocked maybe 10-15 hours total on it. I brought it home, hooked it up to my entertainment center, and I was unable to recreate the problem. Worked just fine a couple of days ago. I just tried it again now, and it won't power up- keeps spinning the fan, then letting out a beep, stopping, and trying again. Like its stuck in that loop again, but there is nothing even attached to it yet! No lamp lighting up, no idea what's going on. If I leave it unplugged, it will probably start working again soon, but this has been totally unreliable for me! I'm hoping I got a lemon and perhaps Viewsonic will do something for me (Its too late to return to Amazon). I will update this review if so. Otherwise, I'm extremely disappointed in this model!
sorry i didn't take a picture before i returned it. i ordered the light grey but it's really dark. much darker than the image in the description.
This product is wonderful. I like being able to cut a piece as long as I need. Good gripping power. Very useful for things other than cables. Love having a big roll.

I have reviewed several similar products and while this could work for some I found the design of this cable management box needs a bit of redesigning.

The point of cable management is to keep your cables looking neat and tidy - this can help achieve that - as long as your plugs are relatively small. While there are four rotating outlets on the ends they are close together and depending on the size of you power adapter plug many will block the plug next to it. I found this was also the case with the outlets located on the top.

This is nearly always an issue with power strips of any sort but when you try to add a cover this becomes even more challenging.

This brings me to the cover design. The side wings expand and must be spread to the maximum width in order to unlock and lift the lid - and even then it’s not easy. This inner cover is thin and not very substantial. Once the other wings cover it that is protected but access is difficult.

Typically I am a big fan of Legrand products and find their designs innovative, helpful and user centric. I cannot say that about this product after testing it for a while.

In real world use I simply have too may power adapter plugs and USB powered devices for this to be a viable solution at my desk. I found it works ok for my entertainment center though I already had my plugs organized there.

In the end, I would recommend one of the more simple box style cord management systems. These allow the greatest flexibility and are definitely far less frustrating to use.

I found the instructions for setting this up quite clear, but despite that there were still things that didn't work. I really think it has to do more with our service here in Appalachia, then it does with this product. I never could resolve the WPS issue. It wouldn't connect. When I moved the extender to the center of the house, I still didn't have a better signal than I do without it. I have a custom built home with lots of wood and tile and the router is in my garage behind a metal door.  I fear there is no help for my situation. I wanted this to work so I could move the Fire stick to the living room instead of the den, but it wasn't meant to be.

This adaptor simply does not fit an iPhone.  Period.  It is not useable.  The plug that fits into the iphone is too small so it is loose when inserted into the iphone and falls right out.  I am baffled that such a poor device would be sold by Amazon.  All I can do now is throw it away.  It is an absolute waste of money.
This is the second one of these I have. I love it. So easy to expand your USB ports with one of these.

I tried these last night for a little. They plug into the same port as your charger so that kind of rules out using them at night while your phone is charging. I was hoping to use them for music at bedtime but unless I change the time I charge my phone that's a no go. Shame Samsung couldn't have left that regular plug alone on this model. Sound seems to be OK in them.

I was really excited about getting this for my hubby last year but it is nothing but a pain. It keeps shutting down and requires a complete reboot to each time you wish to use it. I have not been able to find a fix anywhere.

I hear the Roku 2 is good so will probably buy one of those because this one sure is a loser in all ways!

Here's the major problem with this product. You cannot install it yourself. There is way too much that can go wrong or get hooked up wrong much like trying to hook up a three switch bathroom light/heater/fan. I couldn't figure it out so I called my handyman who was an electrician, and he decided not to do it. Now I'll need to pay an auto customizer to put it in for me.

Admittedly, I ordered this through Vine Voice, so I didn't pay anything for it. However, I thought that it would be easy enough to DIY, but it isn't. So beware.

I bought this to have something to sit my iPad on for Square payments at a trade show, and I found myself using it to draw with my iPad.
It doesn’t slip/fall like other stands and is a steal for the price.
Highly Recommend!

I have been working with security camera products for over 20 years - over that time I have installed systems for businesses, corporations and individuals - from enormous multimillion dollar systems to single camera nursery monitors. I’ve consulted on large projects and written about security vulnerabilities within networks for trade publications.

This is a field I know well. Over the years the technology has improved and become less and less expensive to produce. It is now practical to install security systems that would have only previously been used in commercial settings in residential.

If you are new to home security camera systems, I recommend going with wired (non-WiFi), NVR or computer based recorders run with systems such as Blue Iris. This type of system is infinitely more secure and stable. While initial setup costs are a bit more the security it affords is worth every penny. It always pays to research the products, discuss them with those in the field, understand vulnerabilities and PLAN YOUR SYSTEM PRIOR TO PURCHASING ANYTHING. If you are at all tech savvy at all you can do it. While there is a bit of a learning curve it is not awful and in the end you will be thankful for the time you spent in the beginning as it will save you time, money and endless frustration.

I have set up, tested and reviewed more security systems and individual products than I can possibly remember. Without question my go to solution for home security solutions is a computer based, Blue Iris system with POE cameras run off of quality POE switches. This type of system is not only easy to operate but is highly secure.

As with any component there are a host of factors to consider. In terms of cameras more megapixels is not always better. For instance 2MP cameras often perform better in low light while 5MP will allow for more detail when lighting is good. One disadvantage of higher megapixel cameras is the recordings will take up more hard drive space. Depending on the number of cameras, this can become a larger concern.

I had no issue using this camera with Blue Iris. It was detected immediately and I was able to enable settings.

This camera has a fixed lens not allowing for pan / tilt functionality so it is important to think through placement ahead of time.

Night vision is not great with above average glare from IR lighting so I turned that off and had much better results without it as my other cameras provide adequate IR light.

In terms of price and function this is definitely a camera I would recommend as an extra camera to cover any blind spots but not necessarily as a primary camera.
These surge protectors and well constructed and thoughtfully engineered.  The USB ports are a big plus depending on where you want to use them. Heavy duty cords. They work well!

This looks just like the photo shown in the advertisement and does protect the IPad, once you can figure out how to get it on in the first place. Perhaps they forgot to put mine in, but there were no instructions that came with it. So I had to keep pulling and tugging at it trying to figure out how to get it apart to get the IPad into place. You can see the white stress marks in the plastic from me tugging at the bottom of it.

It also adds quite a bit of weight to the already not so light IPad 6th Gen, which makes it a bit more awkward to hold and carry that it already was. The other issue, is this thing collects every fingerprint, bit of dust or pet fur in the environment and is not the easiest thing to clean.

As far as protection goes, it does make the device seem safer to hand with respect to the idea of dropping it. The edge around the front of the screen sticks out, which makes it less likely that the screen will fracture if you drop the device. There is a good amount of rubber around the plastic encasement and that is obviously shock absorbing, so that is comforting as well. Overall, this isn’t terribly poorly designed, the camera holes fit where they should and everything looks pretty good once the case is in place, it’s just getting it in place without destroying the device or the case that I took issue with.

Instructions would have been useful.

Being both an audiophile and a lover of all new tech when I first read about this technology coming out a few years ago I started looking forward to testing it.

So what are the potential advantages of bone conduction earphones? I’m glad you asked. When properly adjusted they allow the user to enjoy listening to whatever they choose while not blocking external noises, thus allowing the user to react to ambient noises. This should make them safe to use while driving or doing other activities that require hearing ambient noise in order to conduct safely.

Unfortunately these R9 headphones allow for very little fine adjustment. In order for this type of headphones to work properly and provide the highest sound quality they should rest flat, or almost flat against the skin between the front of the ear and the rear part of the cheek bone. When adjusting the speaker it is clear when the speaker is properly adjusted. The adjustment that I was able to do came from working the rubber casing and sliding the speaker further onto the arm that wraps around the back of the head. By twisting the speaker inward I was able to get the speaker closer to the proper location but not exactly correct. Due to this, sound quality is not optimal and surrounding people are able to hear what the listener is playing.

In terms of design choices I find it odd that while it’s estimated between 70-95% of the population of any ethnicity is right handed this manufacturer chose to place the controls on the left side. This makes controlling them both awkward and difficult. While the buttons operate in a standard press and double press pattern it is awkward reaching across and doing it on the left side.

In terms of operation these headphones maintain a solid connection for the full 33 feet they are rated for and the batteries lasted approximately 6.25 hours on the first full charge cycle. Sound quality is middle of the road especially due to not allowing for fine adjusting of the speakers.

With a couple of minor changes these headphones would be a much better product. If the speakers are held in the correct positions it is much better - if an adjustment system were added these could potentially have much higher sound quality. In addition, obviously the controls should be placed on the right side for ease and regularity of access.

This is a great technology with a lot of potential. These headphones while not capable of fine adjustments would probably fit someone with an average sized head better. My head is a bit smaller than average which made these fit a bit too large for me.

Honestly, this is a total gimmick. It’s cheap looking and flimsy. It sticks out an inch from the wall too. The only “neat” thing about it is the cord keeper in the back. But you’d get the same tidiness using a twisty tie. And a more high end look using a simple wood shelf. I would not order this again.

I gave it a three star rating because I think the price was too high.  This is working as expected.  I pull a 32 feet 5th. wheel trailer, and I'm always concerned about the condition of the four tires on the trailer when it being towed.  I mounted the second camera under the trailer facing towards the rear about eight feet in front of the two axles and wired it to the running lights.  Although my installation solved my specific needs, which is all I can report on, I do believe that the system should work in the configuration as advertised.

I bought this for my husband and we've had it for about a month.  The telescope part is great, but last night when we tried to use it one of the height adjustment screws on the mount cross-threaded, stuck, and then broke off.  It was only the second time we'd used it.  However, the company was great.  When we called about the broken screw, they offered to repair the mount if we paid shipping to send it in.  That was something we were willing to do.  When we asked if the screw was a common problem, they said they'd never heard of it happening before.  All in all, I'm happy with the telescope, but the mount caused a few problems.
Fantastic. If you can't stand to have cords and wires all over the place you need this. East to install and works well.

I wanted to love these wireless earbuds, but I unfortunately can’t for several reasons.

The sound quality is not good. The description says “extreme bass”, but there is no bass at all.. it’s all treble.
Second of all the bottom metal piece on the one earbuds fell off after one use (in pictures). They still work without that piece, however these are brand new, and broke after 1 use. 😭

The things I do like: The small, convenient charging case. It’s nice and compact. The other thing I do like is the comfort. The design is similar to Apple earbuds and they are comfortable on the ears, even after a long period.

Unfortunately with these, the cons exceed the pros, and I just can’t recommend.

Even after re reading this “protable”(yes I know that’s misspelled) CD player description I still couldn’t really figure out that you MUST USE HEADPHONES OR EARBUDS to use. No sound comes out of unit itself. Essentially like a disc-man of the 90’s. Bummmmmmmerrr.

I like having a few universal remotes that are paired with all of my components because somehow they always go missing and I hate spending an hour searching the couch cushions for the TV remote in order to unwind.

In terms of an inexpensive universal remote this is not bad. It pairs easily with my Sony TV and stereo and will control most everything. It looks nice and has back lit keys.

Without question the biggest drawback to this remote is the lack of a “Back” button. Depending on how you receive your TV signal or use your TV this can be a fairly large issue. We use YouTube TV to stream our TV channels and having the back button is really important. While I can work around it, kind of, it’s a pain and could easily be resolved by adding that one simple button that is universal on nearly every remote made in the last 10 years.

While this works as my backup to my backup, it certainly isn’t the remote I reach for due to it lacking this critical button. With the back button it would be a 5 star product, without it is a 2.

Pictures have a greenish blue hue to everything which makes people look sallow and sickly. I tied using the blue metering paper before each image but it was the same result. I contacted customer service with attached images (what the picture looked like on my phone and the printed result) but never heard back from them. It didn’t even work to artificially  increase warmth of the image, it still came out with the blue green hue.

So I ended up going back to the 3M Ergonomic Mouse and asking Anker for a refund, which they promptly provided without even requiring me to send the mouse back. Great customer service from them! My review on my experience with the mouse below. My major issue with the mouse was that it was easy to lapse back into using it like a normal mouse - it didn't force me to stay on top of my ergonomics.

---------------------

I bought this to replace my 3M Ergo mouse (the joystick-looking one) that finally crapped out after years of use. I decided to try this because it's so cheap and because people always make "are you playing video games?" jokes around the office with the 3M. So, here I am after a day with it, and I have to say it's about what I expected. Here are quick pros/cons:

Pros:
+ It's inexpensive
+ It looks like a fairly normal mouse
+ It has 5 buttons

Cons:
- Button clicks require a lot of force and are the loudest I've ever experienced
- Back/Forward buttons don't work by default on Mac (but BetterTouchTool will allow you to program them)
- It's really easy to lapse into bad ergonomics and move this mouse with your wrist/fingers instead of keeping your wrist fixed. With the 3M you basically have to keep your wrist stationary since it keeps your arm at 90 degrees instead of 45.

I absolutely hated this product. The sound was always off by a few seconds and it kept disconnecting every few minutes. I could hear the things around me and the noise cancellation was awful. the sound quality was grainy and glitched out sometimes. But the earphones were comfortable to wear and fit my ears nicely.

So one of the reasons I bought this was to be able to charge phones etc since it had such amazing battery storage. Well I was duped.. I have been working with security camera products for over 20 years - over that time I have installed systems for businesses, corporations and individuals - from enormous multimillion dollar systems to single camera nursery monitors. I’ve consulted on large projects and written about security vulnerabilities within networks for trade publications.

This is a field I know well. Over the years the technology has improved and become less and less expensive to produce. It is now practical to install security systems that would have only previously been used in commercial settings in residential.

Personally I really like the flood light security cameras. Flood lights are a natural deterrent for criminal activity - when the light comes on it startles the person trying to sneak around and having the light almost guarantees that you will receive a clear image.

I have tested and reviewed several similar floodlight camera systems. As with all security systems my advice is never use cloud based recording - this opens your entire network to not only the company who you are giving blanket access too but also to hackers. The same is true for WiFi. Obviously WiFi is more convenient but if your privacy is a concern this really needs to be taken into account. Hackers have become quite adept at hacking into WiFi cameras and being able to not only watch your feed but control your cameras and access your entire network. If you choose to utilize WiFi cameras take the time to understand your router and employ proper VPN protocols.

In terms of this particular floodlight WiFi camera by FREECAM it comes with a 16GB SD card which is sealed within and a larger SD card can not be added. Unfortunately 16GB in this application is very little and will not allow for adequate storage if review of footage is necessary. This forces the user into using the cloud service which opens the network to much greater risks.

The app, CAMB, while certainly not the worst I’ve used, is far from the best. It is not particularly user friendly and support is non existent. I was able to achieve reasonable reliability in terms of video capture although the alert system is not reliable.

The bulbs are bright - 2400 Lumen and while they are not replaceable being LED, they should last a long time.

Installation was as straightforward as installing any floodlight and required around 20 minutes.

In terms of WiFi flood light cameras, I’m disappointed with the limitation of the 16GB SD card - this tends to point to the company trying to force buyers into their cloud services. I have carefully monitored all traffic and if the camera was attempting to access the internet independently - my internet blocks will prevent this but I have not detected anything in my testing thus far.

The app is not dependable nor is it user friendly.

All around this is a product I would avoid.

Any backpack that is intended to carry a laptop must provide adequate protection for the computer and have storage for the related accessories.

This backpack accomplished that adequately. There are several internal pockets and sleeves to hold various sized accessories and items, an external pocket for storage, an easy access location for getting to items quickly and a port for charging electronics from a battery backup.

The outer shell material is high quality and construction is well done. The material resists water and will hold up well to use.

Here is where this bag falls short. In an effort to make the backpack compact there is little storage space and it’s just not comfortable to carry on the back. The laptops padded location is directly resting on the users back - this is not unusual in backpacks of this style but with this particular bag it feels stiff and awkward. Regardless of how I’ve tried to shift and arrange items within the bag this backpack is just not comfortable in use. Had additional padding been used between the laptop and the surface that rests against the users back it would have helped as would allow for the bag to shape more to how backpacks are carried.

Once a laptop and power adapter are packed inside this backpack there is little room left for anything more than a few flat folders. This lack of capacity makes it impractical for students who must carry all they need for a day of classes.

While this backpack has several features that are not bad the disadvantages make it a product I wouldn’t recommend.

During the last year I designed a workstation where my desktop computer sits next to my recliner and I have a 32” curved monitor that swivels over to face me. My keyboard and mouse tray swivels to rest in front of me when needed then out of my way when I’m finished. I was looking for a floor stand that would support my iPad Pro where I could pull it to me when needed and then move it out of the way when I didn’t - based on the listing I thought this would be the perfect product.

While the design of this product is very good, clearly the manufacturer did not test it with the iPad Pro even though it is specifically marketed for it. The springs simply are not strong enough to support the weight of the Pro. It will work fine for iPhones or smaller tablets but if you intend on using this for an iPad Pro you will want to replace at least one of the manufacturer’s springs with a 7/16” x 1-1/2” extension spring. Here is what I used - https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008RFNRIW/ref=cm_sw_em_r_mt_dp_Pq.TFbC339YC7

After replacing the spring I had no further issues and the floor stand operates as it should. This would be an easy correction for the manufacturer to make but currently as it is delivered I would not recommend this item for users of the iPad Pro. While the fix is not difficult, when purchasing a product marketed specifically for that unit we should not have to modify the product in any way.

If the correct springs were included this would be a solid 5 star item, as it is I give it a 3 and advise ordering the spring when purchasing the stand if you want to use this with your Pro.

I have been working with security camera products for over 20 years - over that time I have installed systems for businesses, corporations and individuals - from enormous multimillion dollar systems to single camera nursery monitors. I’ve consulted on large projects and written about security vulnerabilities within networks for trade publications.

This is a field I know well. Over the years the technology has improved and become less and less expensive to produce. It is now practical to install security systems that would have only previously been used in commercial settings in residential. Unfortunately while the hardware has improved dramatically the software often has not - which is the case with this set.

The set I got did not come with a hard drive which wasn’t a big deal as I always have a few around. A suitable SATA Hard Drive can be purchased for $25-$400 depending on size and quality.

In terms of this system it should be clear based on the price - this is a budget system. That doesn’t mean it won’t work but these cameras are not going to pan, tilt or zoom (the lens is fixed).

If these are installed with this in mind they can still be very effective they just need to be positioned correctly.

For instance if these cameras were placed up high on a second floor overhang you would see an overview of the surrounding area but would not be able to zoom in to see facial characteristics. On the other hand, if placed on a porch a bit above head height you will have no issue making out the same. Placement is key.

In terms of wiring any POE (Power Over Ethernet) camera is excellent. The cameras are powered and transmit data over the same Ethernet cable. There are several advantages to these systems the most important is security as the cameras are not broadcasting wirelessly making them more difficult to hack.

The camera operation is excellent for the price. The images are clear and crisp and night vision operates without issue.

Where this set falls apart is on the software side - unfortunately this is often the case. The software is so non user friendly that it is almost unusable. I have used countless similar systems and this has to be among the all time worst software operating systems I have ever encountered.

There are countless glitches and bizarre, nonsensical methods for how it has to be initiated that it became comical as I worked through them all. I’ve actually used it as an example with colleagues as why a system must be actually set up prior to recommending it as on its face this appears to be an excellent value but the software unfortunately ruins what otherwise would be an excellent budget system. Unfortunately one depends on the other.

Due to the horrid software this set depends upon I would not recommend it. I can only hope the manufacturer would scrap the current software completely and rebuild with modern usability standards being their primary concern.

It's hard to hate on this drone when it is flying. Mechanically it performs very well in the air. The camera is great & the camera stabilization works very well. The app has been a frustrating experience that has kept me from fully exploring the capabilities of the drone.

After the last app & firmware update we have experienced freezing of the video feed, disconnects from the phone & disconnects from the radio. The good news is that, in the last case, the drone dutifully returned to its starting spot. I didn't have those issues before the update but I did have others.

I have put in a couple of tech requests & their support is very good. In some cases a confirmation email the same or next day & resolution in another day or so. In one case a very nice young man called me to talk me through a gyro calibration.

We have enjoyed using the camera to fly it around the neighborhood. The camera function is fantastic. The attitude adjustment for the camera is intuitive & handy. I'm still learning to fly by the screen so I like being able to "look" up & down to make sure I'm not about to hit something and figure out the location by looking at landmarks & streets.

On the drone platform, the camera can be set to track a person with just the camera & the drone stationary or have the drone follow the person while tracking. We used that to record my daughter's color guard practice. It also has a "point of interest" feature where it flies circles over a particular spot & the operator can start or stop recording while it it is circling. I'm thinking this will be cool for parties this Summer when we can finally have people over again. For now I have is top quality shots of the top of my camper & green pool.

It camera mode it performs very well as a stable, tracking camera. The camera gimble has a lot of range to track a person moving about & the tracking works well. If the person moves out of the frame it will not re-aquire them though in my experience. The camera can be mounted on a tripod or used as a hand-held "camcorder". On a tripod, it kind of hangs off to the side & down so it will interfere with some tripod setups. It is also a bit bulky to use as a camcorder but the stabilization is so good I forgive that. In either configuration a phone has to be used to stop & start recording or take pictures which I do not like & I hope they put out a bluetooth button for that.

The controller is small compared to the ones I used for model airplanes & it is easy to hold. There is no attachment point for a lanyard. The phone is held on a mount that extends out & up & puts the phone in a good place to see but puts a lot of weight up high. The gimbles have a rubber gasket around that makes them water resistant & doesn't interfere with their operation. The controller charges with a mini-usb (ugh) & connects to the phone with included USBC or Lightning cables. The cord going to the phone could be a little more tidy.

The case is nice, thick foam & will hold everything needed to fly except maybe a second battery. It's sturdy enough to put in checked luggage (with the batteries removed of course).

The beginner mode is seriously slow & a good way to get used to flying especially for someone who is learning nose-in hovering & other basics. It also makes a very stable camera platform.

A couple of observations:
I am not great at sorting out things on the app & would prefer robust instructions. I had no idea that the aforementioned gyro calibration was something I could perform myself or that I should have known where to look for it. It took me several tries to figure out how to turn on the tracking features (it has to be "started" after turning it on).

This is my first serious drone so I can't adequately compare it to other models. I used to fly model airplanes & helicopters decades ago & I tend to try to fly these quadrotors like an airplane in line of sight flight. This model seems to resist that more than others that I have flown & tries to force a flat plane rather than let me bank it & swoop around (I hope that makes sense).

I worked in a hobby shop for several years when I was younger (a long time ago). This is exactly what people who came in dreamed of buying; a small, aircraft that could fly in the backyard or far away that doesn't require a steep learning curve to fly or maintain. The camera adds to the fun & makes it useful.

I have a Garmin e-trex GPS as a backup for my sailboat navigation system so my use of handheld systems has been limited to marine use only. At first I thought this Magellan Trinton 400 system would be a good handheld for marine use. Then I changed my mind.

1. Yes, this 400 is in color... but the battery life is half that of my e-trex and I would much prefer longer battery life than a color screen. This is personal bias of course and may not be of importance to you.

2. I noted the 400 was much larger than my old e-trex and seemed "clunkier".

3. However, the biggest frustration was attemping to link the 400 with my boat's navigation program on my laptop. I simply wasn't able to link and their website help page was close to worthless. Never was able to link. My e-trex links right away.

4. I should have been able to download marine charts to the 400 but after several hours on their website, I was also unable to do this.

5. Finally, it was discouraging to google "Triton 400" and find so many negative comments about the unit and Magellan's support.

For land use, other than the short battery life, it might present a decent option. But for marine use, I'll stick with my Garmin e-trex.

I have been working with security camera products for over 20 years - over that time I have installed systems for businesses, corporations and individuals - from enormous multimillion dollar systems to single camera nursery monitors. I’ve consulted on large projects and written about security vulnerabilities within networks for trade publications.

This is a field I know well. Over the years the technology has improved and become less and less expensive to produce. It is now practical to install security systems that would have only previously been used in commercial settings in residential.

As with all security systems my advice is never use cloud based recording - this opens your entire network to not only the company who you are giving blanket access too but also to hackers. The same is true for WiFi. Obviously WiFi is more convenient but if your privacy is a concern this really needs to be taken into account. Hackers have become quite adept at hacking into WiFi cameras and being able to not only watch your feed but control your cameras and access your entire network. If you choose to utilize WiFi cameras take the time to understand your router and employ proper VPN protocols.

Due to my security concerns with WiFi cameras my current security system is completely run through a dedicated computer utilizing the Blue Iris software. While this may seem intimidating to some, it is well worth learning and utilizing for the huge advantages in privacy and security.

I’ve noticed many people have asked if this camera works with a Blue Iris and it does without any issue. Just update to the latest firmware. The remainder of setup within Blue Iris is straightforward. This IPC2387C supports PoE 802.3af and will work with 48V active adapters which are more expensive, but you can also rely on a PoE switch, which in my opinion is a much easier, straightforward option. Something to note, if you plan on powering via a DC cable this is not included.

The fixed lens size of 2.8mm provides decent field of view and the digital zoom is acceptable. The 1/2.8” CMOS 8MP image sensor is decent in low light, certainly not as good as 1/1.8” but I’ve found the low light capabilities average in comparison to other similar cameras. The LEDs must be illuminating objects to provide what is considered “colored night vision” which is misleading.

The camera has a built in microphone that allows for sound recording, though no speaker so two way communication will not be possible with this unit.

In terms of storage, the IPC2387C utilizes H.265 which is currently the best option for video compression. I have had this set up utilizing Blue Iris software which I definitely recommend.

When all things are taken into account this is a solidly build camera, suited to outdoor environments, decent daytime / nighttime resolution, fairly easily mounted, and setup in Blue Iris was reasonably easy and straightforward.

While I would prefer to have pan and tilt capabilities at this price point that is unrealistic. Overall I have been moderately impressed with this budget camera and would recommend it for many security situations.

After using surge protection for audio, video and computer equipment for more than 30 years I can definitely say not all of these products are created equally.

Often similar devices pass my tests initially yet fail after the 1 or 3 month mark - that has not been the case with this item.

The way this is designed allowed me to utilize this in my bathroom to have all of my toiletry accessories charging at once yet have everything compact and tucked away. It’s nice that this doubles as a nightlight that has adjustable levels of light and automatically turns on and off.

The only thing I wish was different was if on the sides the outlets were more widely spaced to allow for larger power blocks - with that being said having 6 outlets should allow most users to have more than enough.

The 3 USB ports are definitely a nice addition as this helps avoid having to use plugs for USB powered devices. There are 2 standard USB-A ports and 1 ultra fast USB-C port.

This is an outstanding surge protector and nightlight that has proven itself through extensive use and testing.

This is definitely a product I recommend.

[[VIDEOID:e88b9f67b8b32ec1c6281d91851e23f3]] After using a rented Crane 2 for a while, I decided to get something more robust for my own. I did a LOT of research, and all hands pointed to the DJI Ronin S being the best build quality for the money, and having owned DJI equipment before I knew it had a solid reputation already. It seemed a little more expensive than the ones I wanted to buy, but then I found this "essentials kit", which forgoes some of the bells and whistles and gives you just the stabilizer and battery for a price that made it VERY competitive!
So I got this thinking it's the deal of the year, until I had issues the first time using it on a job. One of the motors appears to be faulty and would vibrate like crazy at certain angles. I'm attaching the video I sent to DJI's tech support team to help troubleshoot the issue.
Initially I thought I had done something wrong, so I spent some time researching online and tried following some of the balance guides and firmware updates, etc, hoping something would solve the issue. But it remains. Then I found someone in a film making forum mention that he had to go through 3 or 4 Ronin S essential kits before getting one that worked... apparently the first 3 would all spaz out in what sounded similar to this one.
I got nervous and reached out to the seller wondering if there was just a bad batch of Ronin S essential kits out there. I was told to contact DJI because they might have a fix for it instead. Gullible me, I reached out to DJI who sort of gave me the run around trying different things again for them, and proving I had done them via photos and videos (they wanted to see a screenshot of what firmware, etc, what kind of vibrations, results of balance tests), and this back and forth went on for a while UNTIL I MISSED MY RETURN WINDOW ON THE UNIT. I shouldn't have listened, I should have just returned it and gotten another one hoping that wouldn't be a lemon, but I wanted to be courteous and try DJI in case the problem was actually me.
Thankfully, I'm under DJI's warranty so I can send it in for them to properly repair or replace. But to do that, I need to package it up and ship across the country, which could take up to a week, wait 1-2 weeks for the actual repair, then another week for it get back to me. That means close to another month of not having the stabilizer that never worked properly when I bought it over a month ago!
I've seen what it can do, and I'm confident that when I get the stabilizer back it will work better (and I intend to update my rating when that happens to reflect it working), but my experience with this thing so far has NOT been a positive one! There may be a quality control issue with some of the S essential kits, so be prepared to return them and/or deal with repair if it doesn't work right out of the box.

I have a couple of Raspberry Pi zeros that use the mini HDMI sized plugs. I needed an adapter, so I searched Amazon for a 2 pack, and these even say compatible with Raspberry Pi in the title / description.
I can assure you THEY ARE NOT COMPATIBLE WITH THE RASPBERRY PI. As far as I know, the Pi Zero is the only board that uses this sort of HDMI plug (the others use full sized HDMI), and the angle of this adapter makes it IMPOSSIBLE to connect the DC power input at the same time. You can clearly see in the attached image, the HDMI cable blocks access to the input. If it was angled the other direction, it would work fine but in its current design, it DOES NOT work with any Pi board that I'm aware of.
It does work as an adapter for different hardware - my laptop for example has mini HDMI and my cameras use micro. So maybe I'll keep them around for that, but I'm only giving it 2 stars because I bought it based on the claim it would work for a specific product mentioned in the description, and it most certainly does not.

I have been working with security camera products for over 20 years - over that time I have installed systems for businesses, corporations and individuals - from enormous multimillion dollar systems to single camera nursery monitors. I’ve consulted on large projects and written about security vulnerabilities within networks for trade publications.

This is a field I know well. Over the years the technology has improved and become less and less expensive to produce. It is now practical to install security systems that would have only previously been used in commercial settings in residential.

Personally I really like the flood light security cameras. Flood lights are a natural deterrent for criminal activity - when the light comes on it startles the person trying to sneak around and having the light almost guarantees that you will receive a clear image.

I have tested and reviewed several similar floodlight camera systems. As with all security systems my advice is never use cloud based recording - this opens your entire network to not only the company who you are giving blanket access too but also to hackers. The same is true for WiFi. Obviously WiFi is more convenient but if your privacy is a concern this really needs to be taken into account. Hackers have become quite adept at hacking into WiFi cameras and being able to not only watch your feed but control your cameras and access your entire network. If you choose to utilize WiFi cameras take the time to understand your router and employ proper VPN protocols.

In terms of this particular floodlight WiFi camera by FREECAM it comes with a 16GB SD card but while the manufacturer claims the camera can accept up to 64GB, I’ve been unable to get mine to accept anything but the included 16GB.

The app, while certainly not the worst I’ve used, is far from the best. It is not particularly user friendly and support is non existent. I was able to achieve a reasonable reliability in terms of video capture although the alert system is not reliable.

The bulbs are bright - 1400 Lumen and while they are not replaceable being LED, they should last a long time.

Installation was as straightforward as installing any floodlight and required around 15 minutes.

In terms of WiFi flood light cameras, I’m disappointed with not being able to get anything but the included 16GB SD card to work - this tends to point to the company trying to force buyers into their cloud services. I have carefully monitored all traffic and if the camera was attempting to access the internet independently - my internet blocks will prevent this but I have not detected anything in my testing thus far.

The app is not dependable nor is it user friendly.

All around this is a product I would avoid.

While I was a bit intimidated at the idea of making my own Ethernet cables, once I got this set and did a bit of research I found it really isn’t near as difficult as I thought it may be.

If this is your first time doing this here is the cable I recommend: https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01JAVMVQ0/ref=cm_sw_em_r_mt_dp_ADqVFbYVVKVD6
It is important to look for “SOLID BARE COPPER CONDUCTORS” as this will make an enormous difference in the performance of your cable. The above cable listed works incredibly well and will not let you down.

I chose to create my own Ethernet cables as I was running cables to PoE security cameras and I wanted them the exact length so I could run them as far as I needed and not have excess that caused transmission delays.

Adding the pass through connectors and crimping them is quite easy once you get the hang of it. Watch a video and take your time as you begin. This tool is well made and will hold up to years of use.

Unlike the majority of crimping tools this one will allow you to crimp and create RJ45 Cat 3/4/5/5e/6 (8x8) (8P8C), RJ12 6-position (6x6) (6P6C), RJ11 4-position (6x4) (6P4C) and RJ11 2-position (6x2) (6P2C). This is definitely one of the most versatile crimping tools I have seen.

While I was using it I had a bit of issue when stripping the wires as it can be a bit tricky to get a clean strip. After creating several wires I found I got better at this and was able to strip more efficiently.

I have been very pleased with this set and would recommend it.

To summarize the privacy terms YOU MUST AGREE TOO (the fine print most people skip to the bottom of and hit Agree) you MUST give this company COMPLETE ACESS TO ALL OF YOUR VIDEO CONTENT.

Here is the fine print for you to read:

“with respect to Content (including all related intellectual property rights) you submit or make available for the Vicoo Services, you grant Vicoo the following worldwide, royalty-free, nonexclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, sublicensable and transferable license(s), as applicable: the license to use, distribute, reproduce, modify, adapt, make derivative works of, publicly perform and publicly display such Content on the Vicoo Services solely in connection with providing you the Vicoo Services, as permitted through the functionality of the Vicoo Services and under these Terms. You also hereby do and shall grant each user of the Vicoo Services a non-exclusive license to access and use your Content through the Vicoo Services, as solely permitted through the functionality of the Vicoo Services and directed by you and under these Terms.”

If this doesn’t give you enough of a reason to not purchase this product or anything that uses this app read on...

I was trying to setup a studio for a multi-cam live Q&A web-broadcast, and this unit came highly recommended. Turns out this might not be the best choice, and the whole experience sort of leaves a bad taste in my mouth.
First the good:
The functionality of this device really impressed me! All I wanted was a live HDMI switch that would let me keep the audio separate (so it would be live regardless of camera active) and wouldn't blank the display when swapping inputs (which consumer hdmi switches do, as the connection re-negotiates). This unit goes far beyond that, using industry standard production controls for cuts and cross fades, title / graphic overlays, and even some basic Chroma Keying for doing live green screen (that was a surprise!). The little LCD is pretty neat too.
And now, THE BAD:
THERE IS NO HDMI OUTPUT. It only supports SDI, which no one mentioned to me when I said I wanted to toggle between HDMI sources. There is an HDMI preview which shows you multi-view (all the cameras in a grid), but no regular HDMI output. Only SDI, which is ridiculous to me. You have the HDMI port there, at least give us the option to choose whether it is multi-view or program out! Black Magic claims they didn't offer the HDMI out because the connection isn't reliable, so they want to push SDI as the output. But that isn't acceptable, the hardware is there, give us a choice! The result of this stream is going to an internet broadcaster which supports an HDMI input only! Now I'd need to purchase yet another accessory to convert the final product back into an HDMI signal so it can get where it needs to go.
Oh, and speaking of additional accessories that need to be purchased, THE BOX LACKS A POWER CORD. You have to source your own. That seems silly, its such an inexpensive thing that you expect to be there. Thankfully I have a cord from an old lighting accessory that I don't use anymore, but it seemed very odd that it was missing to me. Then, it did not come with a setup guide or instructions, only an SD card. That's fine, going green and all that, but I put it in my laptop and it came up blank. So now I have to find the software and manual myself. And at first, I found what I thought was the correct manual via Google only to realize it was for a different version of their ATEM products.
I know this is an inexpensive product for what it is, but let's be serious for a moment- it still costs nearly $1,000. For that amount of money, how much could it really cost to throw in a power cable and printed manual? $5? 10? Compared to the price of the unit, that's close to nothing. I also know that the buttons on front are because it is designed to be rack-mountable, but when you're setting this up on the fly and try to put on a tabletop, the controls are just awkward. It would have been a lot nicer ergonomically to have some controls on the top instead of the front side. But now that's just nitpicking. At the end of the day, I feel like this device is frustratingly close to what I was looking for, but the experience has been pretty crummy so far.

I have been working with security camera products for over 20 years - over that time I have installed systems for businesses, corporations and individuals - from enormous multimillion dollar systems to single camera nursery monitors. I’ve consulted on large projects and written about security vulnerabilities within networks for trade publications.

This is a field I know well. Over the years the technology has improved and become less and less expensive to produce. It is now practical to install security systems that would have only previously been used in commercial settings in residential.

I have tested and reviewed many wireless doorbell systems. Typically I always advise to avoid any wireless cameras due to the inherent security vulnerabilities but this is often not possible with doorbell cameras. That being said there are key components I look for in wireless doorbell cameras: clear picture that provides a wide viewing angle, it either be hard wired or have excellent battery life, when chime button is pressed alert is sent and app can be opened and responsive in a quick time, 2 way audio communication is clear on both ends, motion sensitivity is adjustable and accurate.

I have thoroughly tested this product over the last 4 months and in comparison to the others on the market around this price point this product has proven to be clearly the best.

In terms of what is excellent - the internal battery is long lasting and dependable - I have found that it will last nearly 2 months on a single charge. The vast majority of wireless doorbells use proprietary batteries which forces users to purchase secondary batteries typically costing $30-$50. This factor is a huge bonus.

The camera is quite good. Images are clear and the wide angle provides a full view of the area in front of the doorbell. The AI Smart Human detection is without question the best I have found. This is a huge feature that helps prevent constant false alerts.

The base station holds up to a 128GB SD card for video storage. This is awesome for multiple reasons. If the camera was stolen the evidence will be saved on the base station within your home and all of your recordings are stored locally. This is an outstanding system.

Unfortunately, as with many similar products, while the hardware is good the software needs some work. As is typical, I can not find any way to force this doorbell to work with better software. It is obvious this was designed on the Tuya platform though I have not found any way of integrating it. I would much rather use the Tuya app where I have the bulk of my automation set but this will not migrate.

The lag from the time the doorbell button is pressed until you can see who is there is mediocre. The FASTEST I’ve been able to get this to work is 18 seconds - and I’m using an iPhone 13 Pro Max with 512GB on a super fast 1G mesh WiFi with a node located within feet of the doorbell inside the door.

The 2 way audio is spotty. It sounds like ordering through a drive through where every 3rd word is audible. Since I don’t use these to have in depth conversations it isn’t a huge deal but should be noted.

This is definitely among the best wireless doorbells I have tested for a while. It is not perfect, the sound leaves room for improvement as does response times but overall this is a quality unit.

While the vast majority of the doorbells I tested I advised to avoid all together this one is one worth using.

I have set up, tested and reviewed more security systems and individual products than I can possibly remember. As with any component there are a host of factors to consider. In terms of cameras more megapixels is not always better. One disadvantage of higher megapixel cameras is the recordings will take up more hard drive space. Depending on the number of cameras, this can become a larger concern.

As someone who works with cameras for a living, I tend to occasionally misplace/lose my lens caps. Having a one-size-fits-all backup like this claims to be, is therefore intriguing to me.
What I found however, is that this fits alright for smaller sized primes (such as those with a front element of 52mm), but a lot of my zooms or larger primes with bigger front elements are difficult or impossible to fit over. Rear cap was also somewhat of a hit-or-miss; I own E-mount, EOS, Nikon F, Minolta A-mount and Micro4/3 lenses, and none of them are too large in their rear element, but some lenses get wide right after the mount and won't allow a cover like this to squeeze over easily. Some of the smaller primes, especially pancake lenses like the Canon 40mm STM, really can't fit both a front and rear "cap" like this since the entire lens would be engulfed in one end or the other.
On top of all that, even if it DOES fit over your lens, I'm cynical as to how how useful it really is. The purpose of a lens cap is to protect the glass from two things: getting dirty and getting damaged. For starters, the material is soft and won't prevent a hard object collision. I'm not exactly willing to test that theory but considering I can press in and feel the glass through the cover, I'm guessing a hard object in your camera bag could still hit it and crack/shatter/damage it. Perhaps the rubber would soften the blow, but a real lens cap would prevent any blow from happening in the first place!
For dust and debris protection, perhaps it is a little useful, however this sort of material tends to attract a lot of static cling. Just leaving it in my gear bag for a bit, I'm finding dust and hair clinging to it like a balloon. Now, it is possible that means the dust/debris will stick to the cap and not your lens, which is useful, however it doesn't give me a lot of confidence to see stuff stuck to the thing I'm about to use to protect my lens from more of that stuff.

I suppose it's good as a backup in my gearbag in case I lose another lens cap and want something to keep dust off it until I get a replacement, but otherwise I'd strongly recommend getting a proper front and end cap for your lenses.

That means the viewing screen is very low, too low for comfort (everyone will be looking down unless they are sitting on the floor). On my old screen, when set up in my living room or outside, I usually start it a few feet off the ground and end way up over my head so that everyone can see. This is just... a joke. It also isn't very stable when fully extended. Using material that needs to be stretched to avoid creasing bends the stands to the point it starts to warp and lean in different directions.
I'm very disappointed in this product. The only plus side I can say is that it DOES fold up nicely into the included carrying bag, but unless you're planning on sitting on the floor and have a very smooth screen that doesn't need to a lot of support on the stand to look right, you're better off looking elsewhere.

I’ve consulted on large projects and written about security vulnerabilities within networks for trade publications.

This is a field I know well. Over the years the technology has improved and become less and less expensive to produce. It is now practical to install security systems that would have only previously been used in commercial settings in residential.

If you are new to home security camera systems, I recommend going with wired (non-WiFi), NVR or computer based recorders run with systems such as Blue Iris. This type of system is infinitely more secure and stable. While initial setup costs are a bit more the security it affords is worth every penny. It always pays to research the products, discuss them with those in the field, understand vulnerabilities and PLAN YOUR SYSTEM PRIOR TO PURCHASING ANYTHING. I have set up, tested and reviewed more security systems and individual products than I can possibly remember. Without question my go to solution for home security solutions is a computer based, Blue Iris system with POE cameras run off of quality POE switches. This type of system is not only easy to operate but is highly secure.

As with any component there are a host of factors to consider. In terms of cameras more megapixels is not always better. For instance 2MP cameras often perform better in low light while 5MP will allow for more detail when lighting is good. One disadvantage of higher megapixel cameras is the recordings will take up more hard drive space. Depending on the number of cameras, this can become a larger concern.

Highlights of this camera include:

Lens: 3.6mm HD Lens provides a viewable angle of 80 degrees

POE: Power over Ethernet allows for one wire to be run to the camera that will handle everything - not only making installation easier but vastly more secure

Images are excellent and even low light images are better than most similar cameras in this price range

Set comes with what you will need (except Ethernet cable and system) to be hung in host situations

PoE compatibility - 802.3af - critical for using with software such as Blue Iris

Great build quality - this camera is well built and will hold up outside in all conditions

Issues I’ve discovered with this camera:

An issue to be aware of with all bullet style cameras is spiders love spinning webs in front of the lens. Just be aware of this so if this were to be hung in a difficult to reach location that will be an issue. With that in mind, any security camera should be hung no more than 8 or, at max, 10 feet high. Capturing a person that can not be identified committing a crime serves little purpose.

Setting the angle of this camera is a challenge - this is done with an Allen headed bolt that is exceedingly hard to access.

Audio is not available with this camera.

With all things considered this is a quality bullet style camera at an outstanding price. The sensor is decent quality, the build is solid and will hold up to years of outdoor use. The lens produced clear, crisp images both during the day and at night. POE wiring is easy and the most secure way to go.

While this camera won’t perform as well as more advanced cameras it is perfect as an extra camera for hard to reach locations. It connects to Blue Iris with no problem and has proven to be very dependable.

This is a product I would definitely recommend.

When I initially saw this camera it checked most of the boxes I look for when looking for security cameras. It is PoE, has H.265, ONVIF support allowing use with Blue Iris, Sony IMX274 Starvis Sensor. Once I received this camera and began digging into it I quickly realized certain important facts.

Most importantly, this is a rebranded Hikvision camera. This is among the companies that has been discovered spying on the US using their cameras - the firmware this camera comes with is long outdated and, in fact, can not be updated to anywhere close to what is current. This is far too risky for me to install on my system.

Any camera you install on your system should have the latest firmware installed and you need to have a good understanding of network security protocols. Cameras such as this one, that are older versions of cameras made by manufacturers that are blacklisted - while still being sold, people who understand the industry avoid them - are an invitation for hackers to invade your system.

While the specs on and price of this camera seem decent - this is not a camera I recommend. This poses far more risks than it is worth.
